species: Orca Breach Cascade -- three consecutive full breaches, single Southern Resident Killer Whale, open Salish Sea
scientific_name: Orcinus orca (Killer Whale, Order Cetacea, Family Delphinidae) -- Southern Resident Killer Whale population (J, K, L pods)
sound_type: Orca breach cascade: three consecutive full-body breaches from a single individual Southern Resident Killer Whale. Each breach acoustic event: (1) Pre-breach: subsurface acceleration (8-12 m/s breach velocity), pre-breach vocalization (excited social calls, high-activity pulsed calls); (2) Launch: orca exits water at surface, full body (6-8m length, 6,000-10,000+ kg) clears surface; (3) Aerial moment: 0.5-1.5 seconds; (4) Re-entry: orca lands back/side, maximizing impact area; impact 140+ dB at source, 10-500 Hz dominant, significant energy to 2,000 Hz; pressure wave propagates through water at 1,500 m/s, detectable on hydrophone at 10-50 km range; above-surface splash 70-85 dB at 30m, 3-5 sec settling. Three consecutive breaches: 30-90 second interval between re-entry and next breach initiation. Total cascade acoustic duration: 3-6 minutes. THE MOST DRAMATIC SINGLE-BIOLOGICAL-SOURCE ACOUSTIC EVENT IN THE SALISH SEA.
ecosystem: Open Salish Sea / Puget Sound. Water depth 20-300 meters. SRKW range: Salish Sea, Puget Sound, Strait of Juan de Fuca, coastal Pacific BC to California. NOAA-OA hydrophone arrays distributed through Puget Sound and Salish Sea allow real-time monitoring of SRKW acoustic activity. Behavioral context: high-activity state associated with Chinook salmon foraging or social interaction. Summer Chinook runs are primary behavioral peak season.
season: Year-round for resident orca presence; behavioral peak coincides with summer Chinook salmon runs (July-September for Fraser River and Puget Sound Chinook). Breach cascade behavior associated with high-energy states: good foraging conditions, social play, cohort interaction. Energetically demanding behavior -- requires well-nourished animal.
family: Delphinidae. Orcinus orca. Order Cetacea. Southern Resident Killer Whale distinct population: J pod (~20-25 individuals), K pod (~16-18), L pod (~30-35). Total SRKW ~73-75 individuals (2023). High of 98 in 1995. ESA Endangered since 2005. Primary threat: Chinook salmon decline from Snake/Columbia River dam operations, habitat degradation, harvest.
milestone: THE MOST DRAMATIC BEHAVIORAL EVENT IN THE SPS ENGINE. Three consecutive full breaches from a single animal: 6-10 metric tons fully airborne three times, each with 140+ dB re-entry impact at source. E=9 through focused behavioral apex -- the single most dramatic thing a single animal in this ecosystem can do, performed three times. The metal summit opens E=18 (degree 337: TAD + Grizzly/Waterfall/Thunderstorm) and closes E=18 (degree 340: Nirvana heavy + Orca Breach Cascade). Breach cascade is behavioral health indicator: well-nourished animals breach; nutritionally stressed animals do not. SRKW conservation: ~74 individuals (2023), endangered, Chinook salmon dependent. The sound at degree 340 is inseparable from the Pacific Northwest salmon recovery story.

coordinates.description: Representative: Open Puget Sound / Strait of Juan de Fuca, San Juan Islands area WA/BC. 48.52°N 122.95°W. Core SRKW range, open deep-water habitat. Primary hydrophone monitoring: NOAA-OA array throughout Salish Sea. Specific J/K/L pod sighting hot spots: Lime Kiln Point State Park (San Juan Island), Cattle Point (San Juan Island), Boundary Pass, Gulf Islands BC.

acoustic_data.single_breach: Pre-breach: subsurface acceleration 8-12 m/s, pre-breach vocalizations (excited pulsed calls). Launch: full body clears surface (6,000-10,000+ kg, 6-9m length). Aerial: 0.5-1.5 seconds, 45-90 degree body angle. Re-entry: back or side landing. Impact: 140+ dB at source, 10-500 Hz dominant, extends to 2,000 Hz. Pressure wave: propagates 1,500 m/s, 10-50 km hydrophone detection range. Above-surface: 70-85 dB at 30m, 3-5 sec splash settling. Total per-breach acoustic event: 10-30 seconds.
acoustic_data.cascade_pattern: Interval between re-entry and next breach initiation: 30-90 seconds (animal reorients for subsurface approach). Total three-breach cascade duration: 3-6 minutes. Pre-breach acceleration signature distinguishable on hydrophone between impact events. Three events acoustically similar (same body mass, same individual), spatially proximate. The three impacts are distinguishable as a cascade rather than isolated events by temporal pattern and spatial consistency.
acoustic_data.energetics: Single breach: 8-10x normal swimming energy expenditure. All energy expended in acceleration phase. Post-breach energy dissipation and reorientation required between cascades. Three consecutive breaches: maximum sustainable effort. Maximum documented consecutive SRKW breach count typically 2-5. Three-breach threshold: maximum dramatic expression while physically achievable without apparent physiological stress.
acoustic_data.e9_calibration: E=9 through focused behavioral apex. One animal. One behavior. Repeated three times at maximum expression. Not convergence (multiple forces), not scale (thousands of individuals), not complexity (multiple rhythmic streams): concentration. Six to ten metric tons, fully airborne, 140+ dB impact, three times. The SPS engine reaches E=9 through four distinct mechanisms; the breach cascade is the concentration mechanism: maximum energy from a single source, expressed to its maximum.

nodes.title: Three-Breach Cascade -- Maximum Single-Animal Behavioral Event
nodes.description: Three consecutive full-body breaches from one Southern Resident Killer Whale. 6-10 metric tons airborne three times. 140+ dB impact three times. 8-10x swimming energy per breach. The maximum dramatic expression of the behavior that is already the orca's most dramatic behavioral option. E=9 through focused behavioral apex -- not convergence, not scale, not complexity: concentration.

nodes.title: Breach Cascade as Nutritional Health Indicator
nodes.description: Well-nourished orcas breach. Nutritionally stressed orcas (documented in years of poor Chinook returns) exhibit reduced surface activity. The breach cascade is an indirect indicator of salmon availability. When the SRKWs are performing cascade breaches, the Chinook are running. The conservation argument is acoustic.

nodes.title: Southern Resident Killer Whales -- Endangered at Engine's Climax
nodes.description: ~73-75 individuals (2023), down from 98 in 1995. ESA Endangered since 2005. Primary threat: Chinook salmon decline. Each individual SRKW capable of a breach cascade represents an increasingly rare event. The engine closes its metal summit with the most dramatic behavioral event from a population of ~74 animals.

nodes.title: Metal Summit Closes at E=18 -- Arc Symmetry Complete
nodes.description: Metal summit opens at degree 337: E=9 (TAD) + E=9 (Grizzly/Waterfall/Thunderstorm) = E=18. Metal summit closes at degree 340: E=9 (Nirvana heavy) + E=9 (Orca Breach Cascade) = E=18. The arc opens and closes at maximum combined energy. The SPS entry (orca behavioral apex) paired with the S36 entry (cultural apex of Seattle heavy music) at the summit's close.

cross_references.relationship: E=9 + E=9 = E=18. METAL SUMMIT CLOSES. Cultural apex (30M Nevermind, commercial maximum of the Seattle heavy music story) + behavioral apex (three consecutive orca breaches, behavioral maximum of Salish Sea marine mammals). Both are the maximum expression of their respective domains.

Both E=9, both Orcinus orca. Echolocation: E=9 through acoustic complexity (sonar precision, bandwidth, behavioral sophistication). Breach cascade: E=9 through behavioral apex (maximum physical effort, maximum acoustic output, three consecutive). Same species, same energy, two different maxima. The engine documents both.

Summit opens E=18 (337) and closes E=18 (340). Convergence E=9 (three simultaneous natural forces) vs. concentration E=9 (three consecutive breaches, one animal). The summit's two E=9 SPS entries are structural opposites: convergence vs. concentration, multiple forces vs. single focused apex.
